title: The Top Five Movies of the Year | The Big Picture
format: The 2019 year-end lists. Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins bring in Chris Ryan and Adam Nayman for four top fives, revealed five to one, then honorable mentions, plus one dishonorable one for Brian De Palma's Domino.

segments.heading: Was 2019 a good year at the movies?
segments.note: a rough first nine months, saved by festival season

films.title: Dark Waters
films.note: Chris hasn't seen it yet, but flags the shrugging 'solid job' reception it's getting as the same muted response workmanlike adult dramas got in the 90s.

films.title: The Pelican Brief
films.note: Chris's comparison for that kind of polite, unexcited praise.

films.title: The Irishman
films.note: His No. 1. A late Scorsese film about aging, contemplation, and death, handled outside the usual gangster-movie register. He saw it alone at TIFF's Bell Lightbox with a rapt under-40 crowd for all three and a half hours.

films.title: Parasite
films.note: His No. 2 (also Sean's). Says nobody shoots a scene better than Bong right now, incapable of a boring shot, and contrasts it with Us, which he calls 'so fussy.'

films.title: Uncut Gems
films.note: His No. 3. On the Safdies' compulsive, hustling energy, and how Sandler has to meet them halfway rather than the movie bending around him.

films.title: Transit
films.note: His No. 4. A period story shot with zero period signifiers, in present-day Marseille. Calls Christian Petzold his director of the decade and has rewatched it three or four times.

films.title: High Life
films.note: His No. 5. Robert Pattinson and Andre 3000 in a prison-colony-in-space movie he admires above all as pure images: bodies falling off the ship, Pattinson with his daughter.

films.title: Parasite
films.note: Her No. 1. An 'instant classic' that already fed into the culture (the Jessica jingle meme) while staying morally contemporary: nobody in it is a flat villain.

films.title: Marriage Story
films.note: Her No. 2, her sentimental pick in a bleak Oscar season. Ends on warmth rather than hope, and she calls Baumbach's movies 'comfort food.'

films.title: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood
films.note: Her No. 3. A love letter to Hollywood and an alternate history that wants the best for people who didn't get it in real life: 'lived in and companionable' despite the length.

films.title: The Souvenir
films.note: Her No. 4, and the only one of the four with it on their list. Joanna Hogg's autobiographical story of a young film student in a bad relationship, anchored by Honor Swinton Byrne and Tom Burke.

films.title: American Factory
films.note: Her No. 5. A documentary about a shuttered GM plant in Dayton reopened by the Chinese company Fuyao, which she calls the likely front-runner for the documentary Oscar.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood
films.note: His No. 1 (tied with Sean). About characters merging themselves with history, the same trick Frank Sheeran pulls in The Irishman. He thinks this one gets recycled and lives on.

films.title: The Irishman
films.note: His No. 2. Sees it as continuous with The Wolf of Wall Street and Silence, all part of 'this late period of Scorsese,' and praises how the first two-thirds set up a devastating final hour.

films.title: Marriage Story
films.note: His No. 3. An interview accidentally unlocked it for him as a legal thriller about people getting carved up by institutions, though he still rates the performances and craft on their own terms.

films.title: The Laundromat
films.note: His No. 4. Soderbergh's Panama Papers movie (essayistic, fourth-wall-breaking, with Gary Oldman and Antonio Banderas), which he thinks is criminally overlooked next to Netflix's higher-profile awards plays.

films.title: The Standoff at Sparrow Creek
films.note: His No. 5, and the pick he had to Google to recall. Henry Dunham's directorial debut, a taut noir whodunit about a militia trying to find the killer in its own ranks.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood
films.note: His No. 1 too. Along with Parasite, the movie he thinks will have a decades-long shelf life, coming up every time people talk about Tarantino, Pitt, or DiCaprio.

films.title: Parasite
films.note: His No. 2. Praises its rewatchability and calls it a rare case of a 'true master working at the height of his powers' that's also just plain enjoyable.

films.title: The Irishman
films.note: His No. 3. A movie about time catching up with you, and the ways you disappoint people while believing you're protecting them. Already watched it twice.

films.title: Uncut Gems
films.note: His No. 4 (also Adam's No. 3). The Safdies' Diamond District thriller with Adam Sandler as Howard Ratner, plus Kevin Garnett, The Weeknd, and Idina Menzel.

films.title: Marriage Story
films.note: His No. 5. Noah Baumbach's most widely celebrated film, anchored by what Sean calls his favorite performance of the year: Adam Driver's.

picks.title: Domino
picks.note: His dishonorable mention. Brian De Palma's little-seen thriller, a movie only De Palma could've made, even if it's 'not necessarily very good.'

picks.title: Plus One
picks.note: A summer romantic comedy he liked.

picks.title: Sword of Trust
picks.note: Lynn Shelton's small indie starring Marc Maron.

picks.title: Triple Frontier
picks.note: Offered half as a joke: a Netflix movie about 'hot dads in vests' she watched with Chris and Sean, featuring Oscar Isaac in a helicopter listening to Metallica.

picks.title: Midsommar
picks.note: Made him 'feel like shit,' which he means as praise for how disturbing and slow-building it is.

Once Upon a Time in Hollywood.title: Once Upon a Time... in Hollywood
